stem: Let \( X \) be a set with [[m]] elements and let \( Y \) be a set with [[n]] elements. How many functions are there from \( X \) to \( Y \)?
solution: Each of the [[m]] elements of \( X \) may be sent to any of the [[n]] elements of \( Y \), independently, so there are \( [[n]]^{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \) functions.
distractor_rationales: This swaps the roles of domain and codomain, computing \( m^{n} \).
This multiplies the two set sizes instead of using an exponent.
This counts only the injective (one-to-one) functions, a falling factorial.
This counts subsets of size \( m \), not functions.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: Let \( X \) be a set with [[m]] elements and let \( Y \) be a set with [[n]] elements. How many one-to-one (injective) functions are there from \( X \) to \( Y \)?
solution: The first element of \( X \) can map to any of [[n]] values, the second to any of the remaining \( [[n]]-1 \), and so on, giving \( \dfrac{[[n]]!}{([[n]]-[[m]])!} = [[answer]] \) injective functions.
distractor_rationales: This counts ALL functions, including ones that repeat values.
This is the binomial coefficient (a combination), which forgets that the \( m \) images are assigned in a specific order.
This only counts the arrangements among the \( m \) domain elements, ignoring the choice of which values of \( Y \) are used.
This subtracts the wrong correction term instead of dividing by \( (n-m)! \).
This is off by one in the denominator's factorial.
This is the count of the complementary, unused elements of \( Y \), not the injections.

stem: Let \( X \) be a set with [[m]] elements and let \( Y \) be a set with [[n]] elements. How many onto (surjective) functions are there from \( X \) to \( Y \)?
solution: By inclusion-exclusion, the number of surjections from a set of size [[m]] onto a set of size [[n]] is \( \sum_{i=0}^{[[n]]} (-1)^i \binom{[[n]]}{i}([[n]]-i)^{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts ALL functions, including ones that miss some elements of \( Y \).
This overcounts by treating the assignment as choose-then-permute without correcting for overlaps.
This is the count of bijections, ignoring that \( X \) is larger than \( Y \).
This subtracts too little to exclude all the non-surjective functions.
This uses inclusion-exclusion with only the first correction term.
This uses a rough per-element estimate rather than full inclusion-exclusion.

stem: Let \( X \) be a set with [[m]] elements and let \( Y \) be a set with [[n]] elements. A relation from \( X \) to \( Y \) is any subset of \( X \times Y \). How many relations are there from \( X \) to \( Y \)?
solution: \( X \times Y \) has \( [[m]]\cdot[[n]] \) ordered pairs, and a relation is any subset of them, so there are \( 2^{[[m]]\cdot[[n]]} = [[answer]] \) relations.
distractor_rationales: This adds the two set sizes in the exponent instead of multiplying them.
This counts only functions from \( X \) to \( Y \), not arbitrary relations.
This just multiplies the two set sizes, computing \( |X\times Y| \) itself.
This excludes the empty relation.
This is off by a factor of 2.
This counts orderings of the pairs of \( X \times Y \) rather than subsets of it.

stem: Let \( X = \{1, 2, \dots, [[m]]\} \) and \( Y = \{1, 2, \dots, [[n]]\} \). How many strictly increasing functions \( f : X \to Y \) are there, i.e. functions with \( f(1) < f(2) < \cdots < f([[m]]) \)?
solution: A strictly increasing function is determined entirely by WHICH [[m]] values of \( Y \) are used, since they must then appear in increasing order, so the count is \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts all injective functions, without requiring the values to increase.
This counts all functions, not just the strictly increasing ones.
This is the count for nondecreasing (not strictly increasing) functions, which allow repeats.
This is off by one in the size of the domain used.
This is off by one in the size of the codomain used.
This only accounts for the arrangements, but a strictly increasing function has just one valid order.

stem: Let \( X = \{1, 2, \dots, [[m]]\} \) and \( Y = \{1, 2, \dots, [[n]]\} \). How many nondecreasing functions \( f : X \to Y \) are there, i.e. functions with \( f(1) \le f(2) \le \cdots \le f([[m]]) \)?
solution: Nondecreasing functions correspond to multisets of size [[m]] chosen from [[n]] values (stars and bars), giving \( \binom{[[n]]+[[m]]-1}{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the count for strictly increasing functions, which forbids repeated values.
This counts all functions, dropping the nondecreasing requirement.
This shifts the stars-and-bars total up by one too many.
This shifts the size parameter of the combination by one.
This shifts the stars-and-bars total down by one too many.
This double counts by also permuting the chosen multiset.

stem: Let \( X \) be a set with [[m]] elements and let \( Y \) be a set with [[n]] elements. A partial function from \( X \) to \( Y \) assigns each element of \( X \) either a single value in \( Y \) or leaves it undefined. How many partial functions are there from \( X \) to \( Y \)?
solution: Each of the [[m]] elements has \( [[n]]+1 \) possibilities (any of the [[n]] values of \( Y \), or left undefined), so there are \( ([[n]]+1)^{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \) partial functions.
distractor_rationales: This forgets to add the 'undefined' option, counting only total functions.
This adds the domain size as a flat correction instead of extending the base of the exponent.
This subtracts one for no valid reason.
This multiplies instead of exponentiating.
This double counts by separately choosing a subset AND a total function.
This adds the domain size to the base instead of just 1 for 'undefined'.

stem: Let \( X \) and \( Y \) each be sets with [[n]] elements. How many bijections (one-to-one correspondences) are there from \( X \) to \( Y \)?
solution: A bijection assigns the [[n]] elements of \( Y \) to the [[n]] elements of \( X \) in some order, one of \( [[n]]! = [[answer]] \) possible rearrangements.
distractor_rationales: This counts all functions, including ones that are not one-to-one.
This is the count of subsets, not orderings.
This subtracts one for no valid reason.
This fixes the image of the first element, undercounting by a factor of \( n \).
This is half the correct count.
This counts a different combinatorial object (a choice of \( n \) items from \( 2n \)).
